name: Illinois Jacquet
abstract:
Jean-Baptiste Illinois Jacquet (October 30, 1922 – July 22, 2004) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist, best remembered for his solo on "Flying Home", critically recognized as the first R&B saxophone solo. He is also known as one of the writers of the jazz standard "Don'cha Go 'Way Mad."
Although he was a pioneer of the honking tenor s…

about_quotes.speaker: Houston Person Part 2
about_quotes.evidence: But I liked Lester Young, Gene Ammons, and I liked Stan Getz and I like Warne Marsh a lot... So there was all kinds of influences out there. ... my initial influence I would say would have to be probably Illinois Jacquet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Houston Person Part 2 interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 2, 1998 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Plas Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: if you’re playing tenor saxophone he should hear Ben Webster or Illinois Jacquet, Lester Young, Don Byas, Gene Ammons.
about_quotes.doc_title: Plas Johnson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Duffy Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: He went on to play with Sammy Davis Jr., Illinois Jacquet, Lionel Hampton and Artie Shaw.
about_quotes.doc_title: Duffy Jackson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 17, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: That was the original 'Flying Home' Band, with Illinois Jacquet (PHONETIC) and Dexter Gordon and that group of guys in the band and my brother was the high-note trumpet player in the band.

about_quotes.speaker: Akiyoshi Toshiko
about_quotes.evidence: This band was my very first bebop band, and they had like, uh, Illinois Jacquet playing, it was called “Jet Propulsion.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Akiyoshi Toshiko — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Hamilton
about_quotes.evidence: we became good friends. As a matter of fact, Illinois, Joe Newman, and myself we became the unholy three.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Hamilton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
